source_paper_title: Shapley–Scarf Housing Markets: Respecting Improvement, Integer Programming, and Kidney Exchange
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Bir__2024, title={Shapley–Scarf Housing Markets: Respecting Improvement, Integer Programming, and Kidney Exchange}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0092}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0092}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Biró, Péter and Klijn, Flip and Klimentova, Xenia and Viana, Ana}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1938–1972} }
context:
Conclusion, page 34, Section 6

As the main open question we left open whether the respecting improvement property with regard to the best allotment holds for a) the core for unbounded exchanges b) for stable matchings in the roommates problem with strict preferences.
title: Respecting improvement for best core allotments in unbounded Shapley-Scarf markets
background_latex:
A (Shapley--Scarf) housing market consists of a finite set of agents/objects \(N=\{1,\dots,n\}\). Agent \(i\in N\) is initially endowed with object \(e_i=i\) and has complete and transitive (weak) preferences \(R_i\) over \(N\). Write \(P_i\) for the strict part and \(I_i\) for indifference.

An allocation is a permutation \(x=(x_i)_{i\in N}\in N^N\) such that each agent receives exactly one object and each object is assigned to exactly one agent. An allocation \(x\) is individually rational if \(x_i R_i i\) for all \(i\).

A nonempty coalition \(S\subseteq N\) blocks an allocation \(x\) if there exists an allocation \(z\) using only objects of \(S\) (i.e., \(\{z_i:i\in S\}=S\)) such that \(z_i P_i x_i\) for all \(i\in S\). The (weak) core \(C(R)\) is the set of allocations that are not blocked by any coalition.

An "improvement for agent \(i\)" is a change of preferences from \(R\) to \(\widetilde R\) such that: (1) \(\widetilde R_i=R_i\); (2) for each other agent \(j\neq i\), object \(i\) can only move weakly up in \(j\)'s ranking among acceptable objects (relative orderings among objects \(\neq i\) are unchanged).

For a set of allocations \(X\) and agent \(i\), define the set of objects \(X_i:=\{x_i: x\in X\}\). Define \(\mathrm{best}_i(X)\) as an \(R_i\)-maximal element of \(X_i\) (i.e., an agent \(i\)'s most preferred allotment attainable within \(X\); if there are ties, \(\mathrm{best}_i(X)\) may be non-unique).

The paper proves that the unique competitive/strong-core allocation respects improvement under strict preferences, and gives related setwise results for competitive allocations under weak preferences, but leaves open a question for the (weak) core under unbounded exchanges.
problem_definition_latex:
Given a housing market \((N,R)\) with unbounded exchange cycles and its core \(C(R)\), and an agent \(i\in N\), consider a preference change to \((N,\widetilde R)\) where \(\widetilde R\) is an improvement for \(i\) with respect to \(R\).

Determine whether the following property holds: for every choice of \(x\in C(R)\) with \(x_i\in \mathrm{best}_i(C(R))\) and every choice of \(\widetilde x\in C(\widetilde R)\) with \(\widetilde x_i\in \mathrm{best}_i(C(\widetilde R))\), one has
\[
\widetilde x_i\; R_i\; x_i.
\]
Equivalently, decide whether agent \(i\)'s most preferred allotment among core allocations can ever become strictly worse after \(i\)'s object becomes (weakly) more desirable to other agents (with \(i\)'s own preferences fixed).

Given a housing market \((N,R)\) with unbounded exchange cycles and its core \(C(R)\), and an agent \(i\in N\), consider a preference change to \((N,\widetilde R)\) where \(\widetilde R\) is an improvement for \(i\) with respect to \(R\).

open_problem_quote: As the main open question we left open whether the respecting improvement property with regard to the best allotment holds for a) the core for unbounded exchanges b) for stable matchings in the roommates problem with strict preferences.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks whether a monotonicity property always holds; a violation would be witnessed by a finite market with explicit preference lists. One can systematically enumerate small markets and check the core before/after an improvement using existing algorithms or IP formulations, making automated counterexample search very feasible.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate objects are concrete preference profiles and an improvement operation; fitness can be the size of the violation (rank drop in the improving agent's best core allotment) computed by solving for the core via IP/TTC-based routines. An evolutionary loop can efficiently explore preference perturbations and market sizes to discover counterexamples or build evidence for validity.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open question concerns a monotonicity property of the (weak) core under weak preferences and unbounded exchange cycles: if object \(i\) becomes (weakly) more desirable to other agents while \(R_i\) is fixed, can agent \(i\)’s \(R_i\)-best allotment among core allocations become strictly worse? The source paper establishes strong positive results for the unique competitive/strong-core outcome under strict preferences (via TTC) and for competitive allocations under weak preferences (via tie-breaking and Wako’s characterization), and it proves a conditional respecting-improvement statement for the strong core when it is nonempty (using the Quint–Wako absorbing-set decomposition). It also shows that analogous monotonicity can fail in bounded-cycle variants, so the remaining difficulty is genuinely tied to the weak core with unbounded cycles and weak preferences.

Foundational work by Shapley–Scarf and Roth–Postlewaite explains why strict preferences are tractable: TTC yields a unique strong-core/competitive allocation, and monotonicity can be proved by tracking reachability/predecessor relations in the TTC graph. Under weak preferences, Wako’s results identify competitive allocations with a refined blocking notion (antisymmetric weak domination), enabling the source paper’s setwise monotonicity for competitive allocations. Quint–Wako (and the graph-theoretic reformulation by Cechlárová–Fleiner) provide the structural segmentation into absorbing sets that makes the strong core welfare-equivalent and allows conditional monotonicity for the strong core.

However, none of these characterizations directly control the weak core \(C(R)\), which can be substantially larger than the competitive set and can contain Pareto-inefficient allocations. The open problem remains unresolved in the provided material: there is no known theorem guaranteeing best-core-allotment monotonicity under improvements, nor a counterexample for the unbounded weak-core setting. Promising directions suggested by the existing toolkit are: (i) attempt to extend the TTC/absorbing-set reachability arguments to the weak core by identifying a canonical “best-for-\(i\)” core selection and proving its monotonicity; or (ii) construct a counterexample exploiting the weak core’s allowance of Pareto-inefficient allocations and the sensitivity of blocking cycles to small changes in others’ rankings of object \(i\).

sources.title: On cores and indivisibility
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the Shapley–Scarf housing market and proves nonemptiness of the core; establishes the Top Trading Cycles (TTC) construction of competitive/core outcomes in this setting.
sources.connection: Provides the foundational model and the core notion whose best-allotment monotonicity under improvements is being questioned; TTC/competitive structure is the benchmark against which core behavior is compared.

sources.title: Weak versus strong domination in a market with indivisible goods
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows that with strict preferences TTC yields the unique strong core allocation, which coincides with the unique competitive allocation; relates weak/strong domination concepts in housing markets.
sources.connection: The open problem contrasts the well-behaved monotonicity of the unique competitive/strong-core outcome under strict preferences with the potentially multi-valued weak core under weak preferences.

sources.title: A note on the strong core of a market with indivisible goods
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes that the strong core is contained in the set of competitive allocations in housing markets with indivisible goods.
sources.connection: Clarifies the hierarchy strong core ⊆ competitive (Wako-core) ⊆ core used in the 2021 paper’s monotonicity results and motivates why the weak core is the remaining difficult case.

sources.title: Coalition-proofness of the competitive allocations in an indivisible goods market
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows that competitive allocations coincide with a core notion based on antisymmetric weak domination (the Wako-core), strengthening the conceptual characterization of competitive outcomes under weak preferences.
sources.connection: The 2021 paper’s positive monotonicity results for competitive allocations under weak preferences rely on this characterization; the open problem asks for an analogous statement for the (weak) core.

sources.title: On houseswapping, the strict core, segmentation, and linear programming
sources.theorem_or_result: Provides an efficient algorithmic characterization of strong core existence and structure under weak preferences (segmentation/absorbing sets) and gives linear/integer programming formulations for core and strict core.
sources.connection: The 2021 paper’s strong-core monotonicity proof under weak preferences uses the Quint–Wako absorbing-set graph; understanding why the weak core is harder requires this structural decomposition.

sources.title: Housing markets through graphs
sources.theorem_or_result: Recasts housing market solution concepts via directed graph structures; simplifies and generalizes aspects of the Quint–Wako approach using graph-theoretic tools.
sources.connection: Graph-based viewpoints (absorbing sets, reachability, best paths) underpin the monotonicity arguments in the 2021 paper; these tools may be needed to attack the open weak-core best-allotment question.

sources.title: Shapley–Scarf Housing Markets: Respecting Improvement, Integer Programming, and Kidney Exchange
sources.theorem_or_result: Proves respecting-improvement for the unique competitive/strong-core allocation under strict preferences; gives setwise respecting-improvement for competitive allocations under weak preferences; proves conditional respecting-improvement for the strong core when nonempty; shows failures under bounded cycle-length constraints; leaves open the weak-core best-allotment question for unbounded exchanges.
sources.connection: This is the source paper that formulates the open problem: whether agent i’s best core allotment can worsen after an improvement for i in unbounded markets with weak preferences.
